sections.heading: What is Counting?
sections.content: Counting tells us how many objects are in a group. We say number words in order — 1, 2, 3, 4, 5... — while pointing to each object exactly once. The last number we say is the total count.

examples.problem: How many apples are in this group? (shows 5 apples)
steps: Point to the first apple and say '1'
Point to the next apple and say '2'
The last number is 5, so there are 5 apples

sections.heading: One-to-One Correspondence
sections.content: Each object gets exactly one number. Don't skip any objects, and don't count the same object twice. It helps to move or mark objects as you count them.

examples.problem: A common mistake: counting 6 when there are 5
examples.solution: Make sure each object gets exactly one number — no more, no less
steps: Touch each object as you count
Move counted objects to a new pile
If you lose track, start over

sections.heading: Tips for Counting
sections.content: For large groups, try organizing objects into rows or groups of 5 or 10. This makes it easier to count without losing track. You can also use your Counter Seeds tool to keep track.

common_pitfalls.correction: Move or mark each object as you count it
Go in order — left to right, top to bottom
